refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across Telemetry and Tasking interfaces during simulated and live teleoperation scenarios. Document network conditions and acceptance evidence.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on control commands.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission protocols tuned to customer network conditions.
DP-C: Establish quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to protect teleoperation packets from congestion-induced loss.
DP-D: Combine DP-B and DP-C: adaptive FEC/retransmission with QoS prioritization to address both link-level and network-level packet loss sources.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining adaptive forward error correction (FEC) with selective retransmission protocols (tuned to customer network conditions) and QoS-based traffic prioritization to minimize packet loss across both link-level and network-level sources.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation communication logic (FEC/retransmission) with network infrastructure (QoS prioritization). This is acceptable because both are necessary to meet the packet loss requirement and are logically cohesive.
DP-D does not strongly couple to vehicle control logic, safety services, or other functional domains; it is localized to the Teleoperation module.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite design that addresses multiple failure modes (link errors, congestion). While it introduces some implementation complexity, it is justified by the high safety criticality and customer acceptance criterion. No independence violation detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer will provide or agree on representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, packet loss baseline) for tuning FEC and QoS parameters.
Assumption: Teleoperation module has access to network-level QoS configuration (e.g., DSCP marking, traffic shaping APIs).
Risk: If network conditions vary significantly from agreed baseline, FEC overhead and retransmission latency may degrade operator responsiveness.
Risk: Adaptive FEC/retransmission adds computational overhead; must validate real-time performance on target hardware.
Risk: QoS prioritization may require coordination with site network infrastructure; ownership and configuration responsibility must be clarified.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC algorithm selection (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and overhead budget; (2) retransmission timeout and retry limits; (3) QoS marking strategy (DSCP values, traffic classes); (4) adaptation triggers (e.g., measured loss rate thresholds). Add a requirement that customer network conditions and QoS configuration be documented and validated before deployment.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the implementation of adaptive FEC, selective retransmission, and QoS-aware packet handling for teleoperation communication. It interfaces with the Network Link to transmit/receive Telemetry and Tasking packets, and with Safety Services to report packet loss events that may trigger safety alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound sensor and vehicle state data from Vehicle module; Teleoperation module applies FEC decoding and loss detection on received Telemetry packets.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound operator commands from Teleoperation module to Vehicle; Teleoperation module applies FEC encoding and QoS prioritization to Tasking packets before transmission.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network communication interface; Teleoperation module sends/receives packets via Network Link and monitors link-level packet loss metrics.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports packet loss events and degraded communication status to Safety Services for operator alerting and safety decision-making.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. FEC, retransmission, and QoS handling are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses module boundary for safety reporting.

Vehicle module is responsible for vehicle control and sensor data, not communication quality. Assigning packet loss mitigation to Vehicle would scatter communication logic across the system and violate separation of concerns.

Safety Services owns safety decision-making and alerting, not communication protocol implementation. While Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert events from Teleoperation, it should not own FEC/retransmission logic.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; assigning DP-D implementation to NGHE Solution would bypass module hierarchy and prevent clear ownership and testing boundaries.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner of DP-D (adaptive FEC, retransmission, QoS prioritization) because: (1) it is the functional domain responsible for remote operation communication; (2) FEC and retransmission are communication-layer concerns, not vehicle or safety concerns; (3) containment score of 95 reflects minimal diffusion; (4) interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and localized.
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module lacks direct access to Network Link QoS configuration, QoS prioritization logic may need to be delegated or coordinated with a network infrastructure module (not currently in the available module list). Mitigation: Clarify that Teleoperation module has API access to QoS marking and traffic shaping on Network Link.

Risk: Adaptive FEC/retransmission parameters may need tuning based on customer network conditions. If tuning is deferred to deployment, Teleoperation module must support runtime parameter adjustment. Mitigation: Design Teleoperation module with configurable FEC and retransmission parameters.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface coupling may create a dependency on Safety Services availability. If Safety Services is unavailable, should Teleoperation module still function? Mitigation: Define graceful degradation behavior (e.g., log packet loss events locally if SafetyAlert is unavailable).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has: (1) API access to Network Link for QoS configuration (DSCP marking, traffic class assignment); (2) runtime parameter store for FEC algorithm, retransmission timeout, and adaptation thresholds; (3) asynchronous or non-blocking interface to SafetyAlert to prevent communication logic from being blocked by safety service latency. Document these assumptions in the module interface specification.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (adaptive FEC + retransmission + QoS prioritization) directly addresses the refined FR requirement to maintain packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific algorithms or hardware) and is verifiable through packet loss measurement. No independence violation: FEC, retransmission, and QoS are logically cohesive and necessary to meet the requirement.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk (Low): DP-D implementation spans Teleoperation module (FEC/retransmission logic) and Network Link interface (QoS configuration). Mitigation: Ensure Teleoperation module has clear API contract with Network Link for QoS marking; document ownership boundary.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-D. The only external interface diffusion is SafetyAlert (outbound reporting), which is appropriate for safety-critical systems. No logic duplication or scattered ownership detected. Containment is acceptable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible: packet loss can be measured end-to-end across Telemetry and Tasking interfaces under controlled network conditions. Acceptance criterion (≤1%) is quantifiable and testable. Teleoperation module can be unit-tested for FEC correctness and retransmission behavior; integration testing can validate packet loss under representative network conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns DP-D implementation. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1, 009.2, 009.3) map to specific DP-D components: packet loss detection (FEC/retransmission feedback), error correction (FEC encoding/decoding), and graceful degradation (SafetyAlert reporting). Ownership and interface boundaries are well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Revise DP-D specification to include: (1) FEC algorithm selection and overhead budget (e.g., Reed-Solomon with 10% overhead); (2) retransmission timeout and retry limits (e.g., 50ms timeout, 3 retries); (3) QoS marking strategy (e.g., DSCP EF for Tasking, AF31 for Telemetry); (4) adaptation triggers (e.g., switch to higher FEC overhead if measured loss > 0.5%).

Revise Teleoperation module interface specification to document: (1) API contract with Network Link for QoS configuration; (2) runtime parameter store for FEC/retransmission tuning; (3) asynchronous SafetyAlert interface to prevent blocking.
Add acceptance test plan: define representative network conditions (latency, jitter, baseline loss), measurement methodology, and pass/fail criteria (≤1% packet loss under agreed conditions).

decision.justification: The refined FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into three sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, graceful degradation). DP-D is a composite design that addresses all three sub-FRs but requires further refinement at the next decomposition level to allocate specific algorithms, parameters, and module responsibilities. Proceeding to the next level will clarify FEC algorithm selection, retransmission logic, QoS configuration, and safety alert integration. The FR-DP-IM gates all pass, indicating readiness to decompose.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time (via FEC decoding feedback and retransmission timeout tracking).

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on control commands (via adaptive FEC and selective retransmission).
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds (via SafetyAlert reporting and operator notification).

assumptions: Customer will provide or agree on representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, baseline packet loss) for tuning FEC and QoS parameters.
Teleoperation module has API access to Network Link for QoS configuration (DSCP marking, traffic shaping).
Teleoperation module can be deployed with runtime-configurable FEC algorithm, retransmission timeout, and adaptation thresholds.
Safety Services is available to receive SafetyAlert events; graceful degradation is defined if Safety Services is unavailable.
Acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss) is measured end-to-end across Telemetry and Tasking interfaces under agreed network conditions.
open_risks: Risk: If network conditions vary significantly from agreed baseline, FEC overhead and retransmission latency may degrade operator responsiveness. Mitigation: Conduct sensitivity analysis on FEC overhead vs. packet loss trade-off; define adaptation strategy.

Risk: Adaptive FEC/retransmission adds computational overhead; real-time performance on target hardware must be validated. Mitigation: Benchmark FEC algorithms and retransmission logic on target platform; allocate CPU budget.

Risk: QoS prioritization may require coordination with site network infrastructure; ownership and configuration responsibility must be clarified. Mitigation: Document network configuration requirements and validation procedure in deployment guide.

Risk: High safety criticality (per risk tailoring factors) requires robust packet loss detection and operator alerting. Mitigation: Validate SafetyAlert interface and operator notification latency; conduct FMEA on packet loss failure modes.
evidence_needed: Customer agreement on representative network conditions and acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss).
FEC algorithm selection and overhead budget (e.g., Reed-Solomon with 10% overhead).
Retransmission timeout and retry limits (e.g., 50ms timeout, 3 retries).
QoS marking strategy and Network Link API contract.
Teleoperation module interface specification (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert).
Acceptance test plan with packet loss measurement methodology and pass/fail criteria.
Real-time performance analysis (CPU, latency) for FEC and retransmission on target hardware.
FMEA on packet loss failure modes and safety impact.
Deployment guide with network configuration requirements and validation procedure.
